questions.question_text: Before supervising affiliated licensees, a designated broker in Nebraska must complete which course?
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires designated brokers to complete Course #6000, a 12-hour program covering trust accounting, brokerage finance, ethics, and risk management, before they may supervise affiliates.

questions.question_text: A client sues over misrepresentations made by a salesperson during a showing. Under Nebraska law, who bears legal responsibility for the salesperson's conduct?
questions.explanation: Nebraska's designated broker is responsible for ALL acts of associate brokers and salespersons performed within the scope of their real estate activities. This includes liability for misrepresentations.

questions.question_text: How many hours does Course #6000 require for designated broker candidates?
questions.explanation: Course #6000 is a 12-hour course that covers trust accounting, brokerage finance, ethics, and risk management. It must be completed before a broker can begin supervising affiliated licensees.

questions.question_text: An associate broker in Nebraska holds a broker license. What limits her authority compared to a designated broker?
questions.correct_answer: She must work under a designated broker
questions.explanation: Associate brokers hold broker-level licenses but operate under a designated broker's supervision. Only the designated broker has full authority to conduct real estate activities and supervise other licensees independently.

questions.question_text: Where can a newly designated broker find official guidance on supervisory standards?
questions.explanation: NREC publishes the Designated Broker Policy Guidelines, which outline supervisory expectations, compliance standards, and best practices for designated brokers in Nebraska.

questions.question_text: A team leader instructs a new agent to skip agency disclosures to speed up showings. Who is ultimately accountable for this directive?
questions.explanation: Teams operate under the designated broker's supervision. The DB is responsible for ensuring all affiliated licensees, including those on teams, comply with Nebraska real estate law.

questions.question_text: A team leader without a broker license attempts to independently manage agent transactions. What is wrong with this arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: Team leaders have no independent authority
distractors: Team leaders may supervise other agents
Only the team's sales volume matters
NREC does not regulate team structure
questions.explanation: In Nebraska, team leaders do NOT have independent broker authority unless they hold a broker license and are the designated broker. All team activities must be supervised by the DB.

questions.question_text: A designated broker learns that a team within her brokerage is using advertising that omits her firm's name. What must she do?
questions.correct_answer: Require the team to add broker name
distractors: File a complaint with the county
questions.explanation: The designated broker must supervise all affiliated licensees, including teams. Team advertising must comply with NREC rules, which require prominent display of the broker's registered name.

questions.question_text: A top-producing team operates across three office locations. Under NREC rules, who must supervise the team's activities at all locations?
distractors: The team leader at each site
questions.explanation: Regardless of how many locations a team operates from, the designated broker retains supervisory responsibility for all affiliated licensees. Teams cannot self-supervise across offices.

questions.question_text: Under what condition could a team leader in Nebraska have independent broker authority over team members?
questions.correct_answer: Only if the team leader is the DB
distractors: When the team exceeds ten agents
questions.explanation: A team leader has independent broker authority only if they hold a broker license AND serve as the designated broker. Otherwise, all team activities fall under the supervising DB's authority.

questions.question_text: A grateful seller hands a salesperson a $500 bonus check at closing. Can the salesperson accept it?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must come from the DB
distractors: Yes, if disclosed to all parties
questions.explanation: Nebraska law requires salespersons and associate brokers to receive compensation ONLY from their designated broker. Accepting payment directly from clients or other brokerages is prohibited.

questions.question_text: Under LB 187, when may a licensee collect a commission from earnest money on deposit?
questions.correct_answer: Only after the deal is finalized
distractors: At the time of contract signing
questions.explanation: LB 187 prohibits agents from collecting commissions out of earnest money deposits until the deal is finalized. This protects buyers' funds during the transaction period.

questions.question_text: An agent at Brokerage A closes a co-op sale with Brokerage B. From whom does the agent receive her share?
questions.correct_answer: Her designated broker at Brokerage A
distractors: The selling agent at Brokerage B
The closing agent at title company
questions.explanation: In Nebraska, salespersons and associate brokers may only receive compensation from their own designated broker. Even in cooperative transactions, the commission flows through each agent's respective brokerage.

questions.question_text: Two agents at the same brokerage disagree on their commission split. Who determines how commissions are divided?
questions.correct_answer: The split is negotiable between parties
distractors: The listing agent always gets more
questions.explanation: Commission splits are negotiable in Nebraska. There is no statutory rate or mandated division. The designated broker and affiliated licensees agree on compensation terms in their independent contractor or employment agreements.

questions.question_text: A salesperson asks her designated broker if she can receive a referral fee directly from an out-of-state brokerage. What is the correct answer?
questions.correct_answer: No, it must be paid through her DB
distractors: Yes, if she has written consent
Only if the fee is under $500
questions.explanation: Nebraska salespersons and associate brokers may ONLY receive compensation from their designated broker. Referral fees from other brokerages must flow through the agent's own DB, never directly to the agent.
